moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with one of the most flexible opening moves, preparing to build the King's Indian Attack system. Nf3 controls the central squares e5 and d4 while keeping maximum flexibility—we can adapt to Black's setup without committing to a specific pawn structure. This move is the hallmark of patient, strategic players who want to outmaneuver opponents rather than clash in the center immediately.

Black stakes a claim in the center immediately, which is a natural and solid response. By playing d5, Black controls the critical e4 and c4 squares, preventing us from freely establishing a pawn there and forcing us to think carefully about our setup.

This is the defining move of the King's Indian Attack—we fianchetto our bishop on the long diagonal, which will exert pressure along the a1-h8 diagonal toward Black's center and queenside. Playing g3 signals that we're building a solid, flexible position where our pieces will work together harmoniously rather than fighting for space immediately. Notice we're not rushing to attack; instead, we're creating a setup that's difficult to break down and adaptable to whatever Black plays next.

Black attacks the center and prevents us from playing e4 comfortably—a common response that fights for control of d4. By pushing c5, Black also gains space on the queenside and signals intent to play an active game, but this also fixes Black's pawn structure and gives our fianchettoed bishop a clear strategic target.

Our light-squared bishop takes its ideal diagonal, where it will pressure Black's center and potentially eye the queenside. The bishop on g2 is powerfully placed along the long diagonal, controlling squares like d5 and supporting a potential future e4 advance or center break. This is the completion of our fianchetto setup—notice how patient and coordinated our development is.

Black develops naturally while protecting the d4 square and preparing to support the c5 pawn. The knight on c6 is well-placed for Black's typical plans, but it also means Black has committed to a formation that we can now attack systematically with moves like Nbd2, e4 (after proper preparation), and queenside pressure. We're now ready to begin our maneuvering—our next moves will typically include Nbd2, 0-0, and probing Black's position with moves like a3 or e4, depending on how Black continues.
